---
title: "<img src='images/UBClogo3.jpg'>&nbsp;Quantitative methods <br>in ecology and evolution"
author:

<!--  output: html_document -->
toc-title: "Home page"
output:
  html_document:
    toc: true
    toc_depth: 3
    toc_float: 
      collapsed: false
---

```{r setup, include=FALSE}
knitr::opts_chunk$set(echo = TRUE)
```

<blockquote>It is empowering to know how to analyze your own data
<br><small>Don Ludwig (2009), former S+ instructor</small></blockquote>

Biology 501 is a graduate course on quantitative methods for data analysis in ecology and evolution. The format is a mixture of lectures/discussions on methodological topics and practical workshops using the R package.

***

## Course 2025/2026 (January 2026 term)

Use the drop down menu to navigate the course pages. 

Select [Lectures](lectures.html) on the drop down menu to see lecture topics and contents.

Select [Calendar](calendar.html) on the drop down menu to see deadlines and upcoming events.

Select the [R workshops home](workshops/index.html) tab to learn about the R workshops.

<br>

### Prerequisites

Students are assumed to have taken an introductory undergraduate statistics course at some point in their careers. 

We will begin at a basic level using a general linear model approach. 

<br>

### Instructor
Dolph Schluter ([email](mailto:dolph.schluter@ubc.ca)) ([website](https://www.zoology.ubc.ca/~schluter/)) office: BRC 218

Office hours: Tuesdays 3-4:30 pm or by appointment

<br>

### Teaching Assistant

Lucia Combrink ([email](mailto:combrink@zoology.ubc.ca))

Office hours: Thursdays 3-4:30 in BRC 104

<br>

### Times and location
Lectures and discussions: Tuesdays 1-3 pm in BRC 224

Workshops: Thursdays 1-3 pm in BRC 224

<br>

### Marks
The breakdown of marks for this course is as follows:

* Assignments (50%)
* Presenting a talk on a topic/paper (20%)
* Moderating a discussion on a topic/paper (20%)
* Overall participation in discussion (10%)

<br>

### Lectures from previous years

Lecture overheads and videos from previous years are on the [lectures page](lectures.html#lectures_previous).
